title: Stripe Sigma vs Lightweight MRR Dashboards: A Solo Founder Take
meta_description: Stripe Sigma is powerful but overkill for most solo SaaS founders. Here's when to use it, when to skip it, and what to use instead at $1k-$50k MRR.
h1: Stripe Sigma vs Lightweight MRR Dashboards (When You're a Solo Founder)
body_markdown:
Three months into running my side project, I spent an entire Saturday writing a Stripe Sigma query to figure out why my MRR number didn't match what I'd calculated in a spreadsheet the week before. Seven hours. One SQL query. The answer, by the way, was that I'd forgotten to exclude a $49 one-time charge from a customer who'd accidentally double-paid and gotten refunded. That's the kind of afternoon that makes you reconsider your tooling choices.

If you're a solo founder somewhere between $1k and $50k MRR on Stripe, the Stripe Sigma vs lightweight MRR dashboards question is going to come up eventually. Usually around the time you realize the number Stripe shows you on the home screen isn't actually your MRR — it's something Stripe calls MRR that includes annual plans divided weirdly and doesn't account for the discount coupon you ran last Black Friday.

So let me just say it up front: for almost every solo founder I've met, Sigma is the wrong tool. Not because it's bad. Because it's a precision instrument for a job that mostly needs a kitchen scale.

## What Sigma actually is (and what it isn't)

Stripe Sigma is a SQL interface on top of your Stripe data. You write queries. You get answers. It costs a minimum of $10/month for accounts under $100k in annual volume, and scales up from there — last I checked the pricing page in early 2024, it was roughly 0.02% of volume with a floor. Not expensive. Not free either.

The thing people miss is that Sigma is not a dashboard. It's a query tool. You log in, you write `SELECT * FROM subscriptions WHERE status = 'active'`, and you get rows back. If you want a chart, you export the CSV and open Google Sheets. If you want that chart to update tomorrow, you run the query again tomorrow.

This is fine if you like SQL. I like SQL. I spent five years writing it before I ever started a company. But even liking it, I stopped using Sigma within about four months because — and this is the honest part — I was not going to log in every morning and re-run eleven queries to see how yesterday went.

And that's the actual use case. Seeing how yesterday went. Seeing whether the Product Hunt launch moved the needle. Seeing whether that one customer who emailed you threatening to cancel actually did.

## The lightweight dashboard category

There's a whole category of tools that plug into Stripe's API and give you MRR, churn, LTV, and a chart that updates without you doing anything. Baremetrics is the big one — they've been around since 2013, Josh Pigford sold it in 2020, and it's still the default answer most people give when you ask. Their Startup plan was $129/month last I checked, which for a founder at $3k MRR is real money. ChartMogul is the other big one, probably a closer competitor to Baremetrics, with a free tier up to $10k MRR that a lot of people don't know about.

Then there's the long tail. ProfitWell (now part of Paddle, acquired in 2022) still offers free metrics. There are newer entrants — I've seen a handful pop up on Indie Hackers in the last eighteen months — that target the sub-$50k MRR crowd specifically with pricing that doesn't make you wince.

The pitch is all roughly the same: connect Stripe, get a dashboard, stop doing math in spreadsheets.

So which one wins?

## The honest comparison

Here's where I'm going to commit to an opinion instead of doing the balanced-both-sides thing. For solo founders under $50k MRR, a lightweight dashboard beats Sigma nine times out of ten. The tenth time is when you have a genuinely weird billing setup — usage-based pricing with tiers, multiple currencies where FX matters, custom contract terms — and you need SQL to untangle it.

Let me walk through why.

Sigma requires you to know what question to ask. This sounds trivial. It isn't. When I first set it up I didn't know that Stripe's `subscriptions` table doesn't include the metadata I needed for plan-level breakdowns — I had to join through `subscription_items` and then back to `prices` and then to `products`. I figured this out by reading the schema docs for about ninety minutes. A dashboard tool figures this out for you, once, at setup.

Sigma also doesn't alert you to anything. A customer churns on Tuesday, you find out on Friday when you remember to check. Tools like Baremetrics email you when churn happens, when a trial converts, when a payment fails. For a solo founder, this matters more than any chart. I got this wrong for about six months — I thought the dashboard was the point. The notifications are the point. The dashboard is just the place you go when the notification makes you curious.

And the real killer: Sigma's data has a delay. Stripe documents this as "typically under an hour" but I've seen it stretch to several hours during peak times. Which means the number you're looking at might not include the signup you just watched come in through your live feed. This is maddening in a way that's hard to explain until you've experienced it.

When does Sigma win? A few specific cases:

- You have custom reporting requirements from an investor or board and need exact numbers sliced in ways no off-the-shelf tool supports
- Your pricing model is genuinely complex — I'm thinking of companies like Retool or Vercel with usage components, tiered pricing, and enterprise overrides
- You're doing financial forecasting that needs to reconcile with accounting and you need auditable queries

If none of those describe you, skip Sigma.

## The spreadsheet trap

Before I wrap up, the third option nobody mentions: just using a spreadsheet. A lot of founders at $1k-$5k MRR do this. Export from Stripe monthly, paste into Sheets, calculate MRR manually.

Don't.

I did this for the first four months of my first SaaS and the cumulative time I spent on it was embarrassing. Maybe twenty hours across those months. Twenty hours I could have spent on literally anything that moved the business forward. The spreadsheet gives you the illusion of control while actually being less accurate than any of the paid tools because you will, I promise, forget to account for refunds or proration or that one annual plan you sold in February.

There's a Jason Cohen post from around 2015 — I think it was on his A Smart Bear blog — about how founders dramatically underestimate churn because they calculate it wrong. The spreadsheet approach is where most of those miscalculations happen. You think you're down 2% and you're actually down 6%.

## What I actually recommend

If you're under $10k MRR: use ChartMogul's free tier or ProfitWell's free metrics. Don't pay for anything. Don't touch Sigma. Revisit in six months.

If you're between $10k and $50k MRR: the math on paying $50-$100/month for a dashboard is easy. You'll recover that cost the first time the tool catches a failed payment you would've missed. Baremetrics works. ChartMogul works. A handful of newer indie tools work too — [Stripe Revenue Tracker](#) is one I've been using recently because it's explicitly built for this size range and doesn't try to upsell me into enterprise features I'll never use. There are others. Pick one, connect it, move on with your life.

If you're above $50k MRR with a weird billing model: now Sigma starts to make sense as a complement, not a replacement. Use the dashboard for daily ops, use Sigma for the one-off deep-dive questions your dashboard can't answer. This is the setup I see at companies in the $100k-$500k MRR range pretty consistently.

The mistake I see most often is founders at $3k MRR paying for Sigma because they read somewhere that "real companies use SQL" and feeling vaguely guilty that they don't. Real companies use whatever gets them to the answer fastest. At $3k MRR, the answer you need is "did I grow this week or not" and SQL is a very expensive way to learn that.

That founder I mentioned, refreshing Stripe 14 times before noon — he ended up writing his own dashboard in a weekend because he couldn't stomach paying $129/month. It broke three weeks later when Stripe changed an API response format. He paid for a tool the next day.

Concept: a complex geometric structure of interlocking SQL-like grid lines and database tables on the left, slowly simplifying into a single clean ascending line graph on the right.
